title: Inorder Traversal
description: Left, Node, Right — the traversal that finally proves the promise: on a binary search tree, this outputs every value in fully sorted order.
objectives: Prove that inorder traversal of a BST produces sorted output
Understand why the BST property guarantees this
Trace inorder traversal on a real BST

sections.text: 1. Recap: The Promise from Tree Traversals
Tree Traversals teased this: "Inorder traversal of a binary search tree visits values in fully sorted order automatically." Let's prove it.
Inorder traversal recursively traverses the left subtree, then visits the current node, then recursively traverses the right subtree: Left → Node → Right.
3. Full Example: Inorder on a BST

sections.title: The output is fully sorted: 1 3 6 8 10
points: Recall Binary Search Trees' insertions: 8 becomes root, 3 and 1 go left, 10 goes right, 6 goes left of 8 then right of 3
inorder(8) first fully traverses 8's LEFT subtree (everything smaller than 8) before visiting 8 itself
Within that left subtree, inorder(3) fully traverses 3's left (1, smaller than 3) before visiting 3, then visits 3's right (6, bigger than 3 but still smaller than 8)
Only after ALL of the left subtree is done does 8 get visited — then 8's right subtree (10, the only value bigger than 8)

sections.text: 4. Why This Works: The BST Property

Recall the BST property from Binary Search Trees: every node's left subtree holds smaller values, right subtree holds larger values. Left → Node → Right, applied recursively at every single node, therefore visits "everything smaller," then "this value," then "everything bigger" — which is exactly the definition of ascending sorted order, guaranteed structurally, with no comparison-based sorting algorithm needed at all.

sections.title: Reading a BST like a number line, left to right
sections.text: Recall the guessing-game analogy from Binary Search Trees. Inorder traversal is like reading a BST's values the same way you'd read a number line from left to right — everything smaller appears before the current value, and everything bigger appears after, at every single branching point, producing a perfectly ordered reading from smallest to largest.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Expecting inorder traversal to produce sorted output on ANY binary tree — it only does so specifically because of the BST ordering property; an arbitrary binary tree gives no such guarantee.
Mixing up the order — Left, then Node, then Right must stay in that exact sequence for it to be inorder specifically.
Forgetting the base case (node == nullptr) — without it, the recursion never terminates.
Assuming inorder is always the 'best' traversal — preorder and postorder each have their own specific, different use cases.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the visiting order for inorder traversal?
questions.answer: Left subtree first, then the current node, then the right subtree — Left, Node, Right.

questions.question: What special property does inorder traversal have when applied to a binary search tree?
questions.answer: It visits every value in fully sorted (ascending) order.

questions.question: Why does inorder traversal of a BST produce sorted output?
questions.answer: Because the BST property guarantees every node's left subtree holds smaller values and right subtree holds larger values — visiting left, then the node, then right recursively therefore always outputs values in ascending order.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Inorder traversal recursively visits the left subtree, then the current node, then the right subtree — Left, Node, Right. Applied to a binary search tree specifically, this produces fully sorted output, because the BST property guarantees every node's left subtree holds smaller values and its right subtree holds larger ones; visiting smaller-then-current-then-larger recursively at every node is exactly the definition of ascending order. This structural guarantee, with no separate sorting step required, is what makes inorder traversal the go-to way to read a BST's values in order.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Inorder Traversal
sections.text: Inorder = Left, Node, Right — smaller, current, bigger, at every branch. On a BST, this automatically comes out fully sorted. No sorting algorithm needed — the tree's shape does it for you.
